name: Square tower silhouette
description: A tall, slender structure built on a square footprint whose height significantly exceeds its width, giving a height-to-width ratio between 1.5 and 4. Walls rise vertically as a uniform prism with little or no setback, terminating either in a crenellated parapet — a battlemented walkway suitable for lookouts and defenders — or in a pyramidal hip roof that sheds rain on all four sides. The single entrance is placed on the ground floor of one face, often raised slightly or fronted by a short stair. Interior floors typically number three to seven, connected by a central or wall-hugging spiral stair. This silhouette is the canonical form of medieval castle keeps, isolated watchtowers, gothic bell towers, and the proud merchant towers of San Gimignano in Tuscan Italy.
placement_rules: Footprint is strictly square (length equals width); any deviation breaks the keep silhouette.
Height-to-width ratio must stay between 1.5 and 4.0 — below 1.5 reads as a blockhouse, above 4.0 as a spire or chimney.
Place the main entrance on the ground floor of one face, centered or slightly off-centre, optionally on a raised plinth.
Distribute narrow windows or arrow slits sparsely along the upper storeys, typically one per face per floor.
Terminate the top with either crenellations (parapet wall with regular battlements) or a four-sided pyramid roof at a 45-60 degree pitch.
Reserve the highest floor for the lookout, bell chamber, or lord's hall — it gets the most window area.

medieval.notes: Rough cobblestone walls with stone-brick corner quoins; crenellated parapet; small arrow-slit windows.

gothic.notes: Tall narrow profile with steep pyramidal slate roof; pointed-arch upper windows; chiseled-stone trim.

renaissance.notes: Refined San-Gimignano merchant tower; smoother ashlar walls; flat or low pyramid cap; regular fenestration on upper floors.
